title: Sets
description: Sets provide O(1) membership testing and automatic deduplication. Master TypeScript's Set<T> to solve duplicate detection, intersection, and union problems that appear frequently in interviews.

sections.title: Set<T> basics
sections.explanation: A `Set<T>` stores unique values of a specific type with O(1) `add`, `has`, and `delete`. Unlike arrays, sets automatically reject duplicates. In interviews, sets are your go-to for "have I seen this element before" checks.

exercise.prompt: Create a `Set<string>` called `colors`. Add "red", "green", "blue", and "red" (duplicate). Print whether "green" is in the set, whether "yellow" is in the set, and the set size.
exercise.starterCode:
const colors: Set<string> = new Set()

colors.add("red")
colors.add("green")
colors.add("blue")
colors.add("red")

console.log(colors.has(___))
console.log(colors.has(___))
console.log(colors.___)

validation.message: Use Set with add and has methods

exercise.hint: Check has("green") and has("yellow"). The size is 3 because the duplicate "red" was ignored.

sections.title: Deduplication
sections.explanation: The classic dedup pattern: convert an array to a Set (removes duplicates), then spread back to an array. This is a one-liner in interviews and works for any type. For preserving order, the Set maintains insertion order.

exercise.prompt: Given `words: string[] = ["the", "cat", "the", "dog", "cat", "bird"]`, deduplicate using a Set and print the unique words joined by space, then print the count of unique words.
exercise.starterCode:
const words: string[] = ["the", "cat", "the", "dog", "cat", "bird"]

const unique: string[] = [...new Set(___)]

console.log(unique.join(" "))
console.log(unique.length)

validation.message: 4 unique words: the cat dog bird

exercise.hint: Pass the words array to new Set(), then spread into a new array with [...]. This preserves first-occurrence order.

sections.title: Type-safe intersection and union
sections.explanation: Set intersection (common elements) and union (all elements) are frequent interview operations. Iterate one set and check membership in the other. TypeScript's generics ensure you only compare compatible types.

exercise.prompt: Write a generic `union<T>` function that returns the union of two sets. Write a generic `difference<T>` function that returns elements in the first set but not the second. Test with sets {1,2,3} and {2,3,4}.

validation.message: Use generic type parameter and Set methods

exercise.hint: Union: start with a copy of set a, then add all elements from b. Difference: include only elements from a that are NOT in b.

sections.title: Contains duplicate pattern
sections.explanation: A classic interview problem: detect if any value appears more than once. Using a Set, iterate the array — if you've seen the element, return true; otherwise add it. This is O(n) time and O(n) space, the optimal solution interviewers expect.

exercise.prompt: Write a function `firstDuplicate` that returns the first value that appears twice in a number array, or -1 if no duplicates exist. Print results for [2,1,3,5,3,2] and [1,2,3,4].

validation.message: First duplicate is 3, second array has no duplicates
Use a Set with has/add to track seen elements

exercise.hint: Check has() before add(). The first element that's already in the set is the first duplicate.
